coredata.dc:title: Weathered coal-based carbon dots modified by organic amine for enhanced crystallinity and toughness of poly(lactic acid) film
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yumiao
Jilili, Yikelamu
Shao, Tengfei
Zhen, Weijun
coredata.dc:description: Poly(lactic acid) (PLA) has its own limitations in terms of slow crystallization rate and low crystallinity during processing, resulting in poor toughness and thermal stability, which seriously restricts the practical application of PLA. Blending nanoparticles into the PLA matrix is an effective way to improve PLA crystallization. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by green oxidation using weathered coal as carbon source and then surface-modified with dodecylamine (DDA) and octadecylamine (ODA). Modified CDs (MCDs)/PLA composite films were prepared using MCDs as filler to improve the crystallinity and toughness of PLA films. The results showed that the improvement effect of ODA-modified CDs (ODACDs) was better than that of DDA-modified CDs (DDACDs). The crystallinity of PLA composite film (0.05 wt% ODACDs) was increased from 7.20% (pure PLA film) to 35.44%, and its elongation at break was increased by 5.01 times compared with that of the pure PLA film. Moreover, thermogravimetric analysis suggested that the thermal stability of MCDs/PLA films was also improved. The results of simultaneous rheology and in-situ FTIR analysis as well as molecular dynamics simulations confirmed that MCDs had a strong interaction with PLA molecules, which promoted the crystallization of PLA film, thereby improving its toughness and thermal stability.

head.ce:title: Weathered coal-based carbon dots modified by organic amine for enhanced crystallinity and toughness of poly(lactic acid) film

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poly(lactic acid) (PLA) has its own limitations in terms of slow crystallization rate and low crystallinity during processing, resulting in poor toughness and thermal stability, which seriously restricts the practical application of PLA. Blending nanoparticles into the PLA matrix is an effective way to improve PLA crystallization. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by green oxidation using weathered coal as carbon source and then surface-modified with dodecylamine (DDA) and octadecylamine (ODA). Modified CDs (MCDs)/PLA composite films were prepared using MCDs as filler to improve the crystallinity and toughness of PLA films. The results showed that the improvement effect of ODA-modified CDs (ODACDs) was better than that of DDA-modified CDs (DDACDs). The crystallinity of PLA composite film (0.05 wt% ODACDs) was increased from 7.20% (pure PLA film) to 35.44%, and its elongation at break was increased by 5.01 times compared with that of the pure PLA film. Moreover, thermogravimetric analysis suggested that the thermal stability of MCDs/PLA films was also improved. The results of simultaneous rheology and in-situ FTIR analysis as well as molecular dynamics simulations confirmed that MCDs had a strong interaction with PLA molecules, which promoted the crystallization of PLA film, thereby improving its toughness and thermal stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing consumption of fossil fuel-based polymers has created significant environmental concerns and impacts. Poly(lactic acid) (PLA), a biodegradable polymer material, has great development potential due to the characteristics of renewable, nontoxic, and processable [1–3]. However, the inherent slow crystallization rate and low crystallinity in PLA processing lead to its poor toughness, poor thermal stability, and other problems, which limit its production and application in mulch film, packaging, and others [3]. Consequently, extensive studies have recently been focused on improving PLA crystallization to improve its toughness and thermal stability [4,5].

Recent studies have shown that the most effective way to improve PLA crystallization is to introduce nanoparticles into the PLA matrix as nucleating agents [6]. It is noteworthy that carbon materials have unique advantages as heterogeneous nucleating agents [7–10]. Park et al. [8] demonstrated that the addition of carbon nanotube (CNT) effectively increased the crystallization rate of PLA/CNT nanocomposites using heterogeneous nucleation. Jiang et al. [9] prepared two different sizes of graphene oxide (GO)/PLA composites and found that the bigger size of GO could promote crystal growth and improve the crystallization ability of PLA. Likewise, carbon microspheres were used to improve the crystallization properties of PLA [10]. However, due to the poor affinity of nanoparticles with PLA matrix, nanoparticles shows an obvious tendency to agglomerate upon incorporation with PLA, which will become stress concentration points and thus detrimental to the terminal mechanical properties of composites [11]. Therefore, the development of efficient surface modification of nanoparticles is particularly important, which not only conquers the above drawback but also retains the initial characteristics of nanoparticles [11–13]. Luo et al. [12] reported that amino-functionalized nano-SiO2 (m@g-SiO2) was synthesized through coupling reaction on the surface, and the mechanical properties and crystallization behavior of PLA/m@g-SiO2 nanocomposites were increased compared to neat PLA, indicating that the organically surface modification enhances the interfacial interaction between organic polymers and inorganic SiO2. Nanodiamond improves its interfacial adhesion with PLA matrix by grafting with acid-amine, realizing uniform dispersion and effectively improving the toughness and thermal stability of PLA [13]. These studies have made significant progress in improving the interface interaction of nanoparticles with PLA through surface modification. However, there have been no reports on the interfacial compatibility between CDs by surface modification and PLA.

Carbon dots (CDs), also known as fluorescent CDs, carbon quantum dots, or carbon nanodots, is one of the zero nanomaterials which has many potential applications, due to its various advantages including photoluminescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and easy surface functionalization [14–18]. Due to its excellent properties, some scholars have recently tried to incorporate CDs with PLA to achieve the functional application of PLA. Yu et al. [19] introduced CDs into PLA to create a novel luminescent composite that can be used for food labeling, tracking, packaging, and production of origin. Cui et al. [20] reported a new solid-state biomass fluorescence nanofiber membrane composited CDs with PCL/PLA, which was used as fluorescence sensors for imaging and biosensing of Cu2+. The incorporation of CDs within PLA scaffolds leads to enhanced cellular adhesion, growth, proliferation, osteoblast differentiation, and extracellular mineralization in comparison with pure PLA scaffolds [21]. These studies have made progress in enriching the functional application of PLA. However, there have been no reports on the effect of modified CDs (MCDs) on the crystallinity and toughness of PLA.

Coal and its derivatives, as a cheap and readily available carbon precursor, are considered to be preferable for large-scale production of CDs because of nanometer-sized crystalline carbon domains inherent in the structure [22], which can be facilely exfoliated into nanometer-size CDs by chemical oxidation at low cost [23]. There is abundant weathered coal in China [24]. As for its high oxygen content and low calorific value, the majority of the weathered coal has not been used as a resource but a solid waste. Even if a small minority of weathered coal is used to extract humic acid, strong acids or bases will be used, which will also cause serious pollution problems [25–27]. Therefore, the use of weathered coal as a carbon source to prepare CDs can not only realize the utilization of waste resources but also reduce environmental pollution.

In recent years, with the development of computational technology, molecular dynamics (MD) plays an increasingly important role in the study of biopolymers and their composites. Jolfaei et al. [28] calculated the thermal properties of deoxyribonucleic acid with precise atomic arrangements by equilibrium and non-equilibrium MD methods. Maleki et al. [29] used MD to study the adsorption and release behavior of drugs from CNT. Li et al. [30] simulated the structure of PLA porous scaffolds using ABAQUS analysis method and extracted their mechanical and physical properties. Ren et al. [31] used MD to simulate the interfacial adhesion behavior of PLA on cellulose surfaces. The above research results indicate that MD is an appropriate and reliable method to analyze the structure, properties, and interactions of biopolymers and their composites.

In this work, the weathered coal was used as a precursor to prepare CDs via the green oxidation method. MCDs was then synthesized by amidating CDs with two organic ammonia molecules to improve its compatibility with PLA molecules. The goal is to blend the obtained MCDs with PLA to prepare MCDs/PLA films with higher crystallinity, toughness, and thermal stability. In addition, the structure-property relationship of MCDs/PLA composite films was studied by simultaneous rheology and in-situ FTIR technology and MD simulations, which are expected to reveal the interaction mechanism between MCDs and PLA at a molecular scale.

Weathered coal (Table S1 lists the proximate and ultimate analyses of weathered coal) was provided by Xinjiang branch of Shandong Chuangxin Humic Acid Technology Co., Ltd., China. PLA (injection grade, Mw=100,000 g∙mol−1) was purchased from Shenzhen Guanghuaweiye Industrial Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China. Dodecylamine (DDA), octadecylamine (ODA), and triethylamine (TEA) are analytical reagents and were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) are analytical reagents and were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, formic acid (FA), and hydrogen peroxide (30%) are analytical reagents and were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Proteinase K was purchased from Merck KgaA, Darmstadt, Germany. All the chemical reagents were used as received without any purification.

The weathered coal was pulverized by a ball grinder to pass through a 200-mesh sieve and then dried in a vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h before use. About 2 g of dried weathered coal was mixed with 120 mL of H2O2/HCOOH (1,1) solution in a 250 mL beaker and ultrasonically dispersed for 0.5 h. The resulting mixture was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 24 h, followed by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove the residue. The supernatant was evaporated to dryness on the rotary evaporator. The solid left on the wall of the evaporation bottle was washed down with distilled water and freeze-dried to obtain dark brown weathered coal-based CDs.

In a typical run, CDs (0.10 g), NHS (0.14 g), and EDC (0.11 g) were ultrasonically dispersed in 50 mL of DMF for 30 min, after that the mixture was transferred to a water bath with an indicated temperature and magnetic stirring. Subsequently, a certain amount of DDA or ODA was added, the pH was adjusted to 8 by TEA and maintained at 45 °C for 8.5 h. The reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm to remove the supernatant. The solid was washed ultrasonically three times with ethanol and distilled water, respectively, and freeze-dried to prepare CDs modified by DDA or ODA, abbreviated as DDACDs or ODACDs. The synthesis scheme is shown in Fig. 1 , and the experimental conditions presented in Table S2 were optimized using the response surface methodology.

Firstly, PLA was dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h before use. PLA was mixed with 0, 0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.3 wt%, and 0.5 wt% of DDACDs or ODACDs, respectively, and then processed through a film blowing machine with twin screw extrusion (Poly QC600, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to obtain the MCDs/PLA films with a thickness of 0.02–0.03 mm. In the processing, the temperature of the feed section, blending section, homogenizing section, and the die were 175 °C, 180 °C, 180 °C and 180 °C, respectively. The extruder screw rotation speed was 15 rpm, the roller rotation was 14 rpm, and the airflow rate was 500 mL∙min‐ −1.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was performed with a JEM-2100F plus electron microscope at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken on a Thermo Fisher Scientific EscaLab 250Xi spectrophotometer with the Al Ka radiation and the beam spot size of 500 mm. Accurate binding energies could be determined by referring to the C1s peak at 284.8 eV. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV 3600 Ultraviolet Spectrograph, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were conducted on a Duetta fluorescence spectrometer, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. The water contact angle (WCA) of the sample was analyzed with a Dataphysics OCA15EC contact angle meter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance testing of PLA and MCDs/PLA films
ce:section.ce:para:
The tensile properties of the pure PLA and MCDs/PLA films were tested by an INSTRON 5967 electronic universal testing machine according to ASTM Standard D638. Each sample was tested at least five times and the average of three tests was recorded. The fracture surface of films was observed using a Hitachi SU8010 field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The elemental composition and mapping of the film surface were measured by the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) analysis. FTIR analyses of the films were performed on ATR-FTIR (VERTEX 70, Bruker, Germany) and UV–vis transmission spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV 3600 ultraviolet spectrometer. The crystallinity and thermal performance of films were carried out on a TA Q2000 differential scanning calorimeter (DSC), increasing from 30 °C to 185 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C∙min−1 and N2 flow rate of 50 mL∙min−1. The crystallinity (Xc) of samples can be obtained by the following equation [32]:

where the ∆Hm 0 of pure PLA is 93.7 J·g−1 and f is the mass fraction of pure PLA. Photographs of isothermal crystallization of the films at 130 °C were taken with a polarized light microscope (POM) instrument (DM2700P, Leica, Germany). The thermal stability of the films was determined on a TA SDT-Q600 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) from 30 °C to 600 °C with 10 °C∙min−1 under N2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous rheology and in-situ FTIR testing
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the relationship between molecular structure changes and polymer melting properties, simultaneous rheological and FTIR measurements were performed on a Rheonaut rheometer (Thermo Scientific HAAKE, MARS 60, USA) and a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Different PLA films were molded into discs (35 mm diameter and 1 mm thickness) and held at 180 °C for 5 min to eliminate thermal history, then the temperature was rapidly reduced to 135 °C, and the isothermal crystallization was carried out at this temperature for 1 h. During the isothermal crystallization process, the FTIR spectrum and rheological behavior were simultaneously collected. The relative crystallinity was calculated according to Eq. (2) [33].

The MD simulations were all carried out on Materials Studio 6.0. First, a PLA model with 60 repeating units, CDs model and MCDs model were constructed, and the MCDs/PLA composite model was constructed on the basis of the above three models (Fig. 2 ). The CDs model was constructed according to the characterizations and formation mechanism of CDs, and the initial density of the MCDs/PLA composite model were 1.20 g∙cm−3. Then, a series of MD simulations on the PLA and MCDs/PLA composite models were performed in the Forcite module to obtain the final equilibrium model for analysis. The specific and sequential simulation steps are geometry optimization, annealing treatment and kinetic relaxation, and the force field used in the simulations is COMPASS. The annealing process consists of 20 cycles and each cycle involves increasing the temperature from 0 °C to 200 °C followed by cooling to 0 °C. The kinetic relaxation was performed in the NPT (T=0 °C, P=1 bar) and NVT (T=0 °C, time step=1 fs) system for 300 ps, respectively, in which the temperature controller is velocity scale and the pressure controller is Berendsen.

The binding energy (denoted as Ebinding) is used to calculate the strength of the interaction between the MCDs and PLA.

The biodegradability of PLA-based films was studied by proteinase K. Firstly, the PLA-based films were cut into 5 cm × 5 cm samples, weighed, placed in beakers and immersed into 10 mL proteinase K solution with a concentration of 0.2 mg∙mL−1 (pH=8.5). The beakers were then put into a shaker incubator with a temperature of 50 °C and a rotation speed of 150 rpm. After degradation for a period of time, the samples were taken out, washed and dried, and weighed. Each sample after weighing was analyzed with FTIR, SEM, Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) (HLC-8320GPC, TOSOH, Japan) and DSC. As a comparison, the original PLA-based films were also analyzed with the same characterization methods above. GPC analysis was performed using tetrahydrofuran as the eluent (flow rate: 0.6 mL∙min−1) and polystyrene standards for calibration.

All experiments were conducted independently at least three times, data were analyzed by variance analysis, and mean comparisons were performed using Tukey's test to determine the 95% confidence intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and formation mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table S1, Qitai weathered coal (QWC) has high oxygen content and high volatile content, which could be caused by weathering [36]. Fig. 3c shows the obvious CO, CO and -OH signals, suggesting that a relatively large number of oxygen-containing functional groups were formed during the weathering process. The high oxidative nature of QWC ensures its high reactivity in reaction with H2O2 [37,38].

The TEM image (Fig. 4 ) illustrated that CDs were well dispersed without apparent aggregation, and their sizes mainly range from 1 nm to 4 nm in diameter and feature an average diameter of 2.29 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image revealed that these nanoparticles possess crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.21 nm, which is close to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite carbon (Fig. 4c).

Fig. S1 depicted that the CDs exhibited an absorbance peak at about 251 nm on account of the π−π* transitions of the aromatic π system and an unapparent shoulder peak at around 300 nm in connection with the n−π* transition of the CO or CO bonds [39]. Under UV light at 365 nm, the aqueous solution of CDs can emit cyan fluorescence (in the inset photo of Fig. S1), which is a special feature of CDs [39]. Fig. 2b presented the PL spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 420 to 500 nm and the maximum PL intensity was observed at 480 nm.

Similar to QWC, the FTIR spectrum of CDs shown the presence of -OH, CO, CC and CO peaks (Fig. 3c). However, the peaks of -OH, CO, and CO functional groups were enhanced in the spectrum of CDs, indicating the generation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups during the chemical oxidation of QWC [40].

The chemical composition of CDs was determined by XPS. It can be seen that the prepared CDs feature a C1s peak at 284.4 eV, an O1s peak at 531.0 eV, and an N1s peak at 398.2 eV (Fig. 5a). Atomic percentages of the C, O and N elements in CDs were calculated to be 47.19%, 48.93% and 3.88%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 5b) discloses the presence of sp2 CC (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (286.2 eV) and CO (288.8 eV) [41], which is consistent with the FTIR analysis.

Based on the above observations, a possible synthesis mechanism is proposed, as shown in Fig. 3a. The large-area conjugated structure of coal was broken by weathering to small crystalline carbon domains weakly linked by aliphatic chains, which provides an essential premise for the subsequent mild oxidation at room temperature. The •OH radical, which is derived from H2O2 and considered to be an effective oxidizing species [42], can selectively oxidize the aliphatic bridge bonds and alkyl side chains in the macromolecular structure of weathered coal, while crystalline carbon domains remain intact. Furthermore, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are formed at the oxidation fracture site, and CO2 and H2O are released, thus leading to the formation of CDs with small crystalline carbon domains and enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups. The addition of the FA, especially the appropriate proportion of FA, can enhance the oxidation ability of H2O2 [41], which is also the reason that CDs can be successfully prepared at room temperature via chemical oxidation. The safety of H2O2 and the non-renewability of weathered coal may limit the industrial application of the method for preparing CDs, and the reaction mechanism is helpful to find alternative oxidants and raw materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation optimization and characterization of MCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because the surface of CDs is rich in oxygen-bearing functional groups, it shows an obvious tendency to agglomerate upon incorporation in PLA, resulting in poor performance of the PLA composite. However, if the carboxyl of CDs surface is modified by an amide coupling reaction with organic amine to enhance the interfacial interaction between PLA and CDs, the above drawback will be conquered effectively [43]. For this reason, to understand the differences in specific influencing factors for surface modification of CDs, the following systematic experimental studies were carried out.

Four single factors including reaction temperature (A), molar ratio of EDC to NHS (B), amount of ODA (C) and reaction time (D) were investigated to determine the factor level (Fig. S2) and optimize the preparation conditions to obtain the best mass yield of MCDs. Therefore, with the mass yield of the MCDs as the objective function, the optimization experiment of the above four factors was designed by Box-Behnken design (BBD) software, and the response surface model was used for optimization [44]. The experimental results are shown in Table S3. In the variance analysis of the response surface experiment shown in Table S4, the order of influence on the mass yield of MCDs is C>B>A>D, in which the interaction between C and D has the greatest influence (Fig. S3). The results were verified according to the predicted optimum reaction conditions (amount of ODA of 0.25 g, molar ratio of EDC to NHS of 3:4, reaction temperature of 45 °C, reaction time of 8.5 h, and mass ratio of MCDs to CDs of 195.70 %) and the absolute values of errors verified by three experiments are all within 5%.

The WCA testing results of the MCDs (Fig. 6 ) shown that DDACDs and ODACDs have a WCA of 87.9° and 100.9°, respectively, which significantly reduce the hydrophilicity of the water-soluble CDs (27.7°) and thus improve the compatibility with PLA molecules. According to the FTIR spectra in Fig. 3c, DDACDs and ODACDs show new obvious vibration peaks of long-chain hydrocarbons at 2800–3000 cm−1 and the new appearance of vibration bands at 1625 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 correspond to the amide I and amide II bands [32], compared to CDs. In addition, the peaks of CO functional group weaken in the spectra of the DDACDs and ODACDs. All the above analyses suggested that the surface modification of CDs was successful. After modification with amines, atomic percentages of the N element increase while that of O element reduce in DDACDs and ODACDs (Fig. 5a), as also the relative ratio of CO to CC bond is significantly decreased in the high-resolution C1s spectrum of DDACDs and ODACDs (Fig. 5c and d), which further confirmed that the modification of CDs was successful [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of performances for MCDs/PLA films
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of MCDs content on the tensile strength and elongation at break of PLA-based films was illustrated in Fig. 7 , and the tensile stress-strain curves of pure PLA film and MCDs/PLA films were shown in Fig. S4. The pure PLA film only showed an elongation at break of 19.43%, indicating the typical brittle behavior. With 0.1 wt% addition of DDACDs, the elongation at break of DDACDs/PLA film reached the maximum of 67.84%. Nevertheless, the elongation at break of ODACDs/PLA film reached 97.29% with only 0.05 wt% of ODACDs, and such noticeable enhancement of elongation at break reflects the uniform dispersion of ODACDs with small size in the PLA film and the strong interaction between them, which facilitates the effective tension transfer from the PLA matrix to the high strength ODACDs, and achieves the effect of toughening [45,46]. Moreover, since the aliphatic chain of ODA is longer than that of DDA, ODACDs may have better compatibility and stronger interaction with PLA macromolecules than DDACDs, which will be verified in later analysis. However, the elongation at break of DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA film decreased with further increase in DDACDs and ODACDs content. This might be explained by the fact that the excessive MCDs could act as stress concentration points in PLA composite film, and the similar phenomenon has been reported previously in other PLA composites [12]. The effect of MCDs contents on the tensile strength of PLA films is relatively small compared to the changes of the elongation at break. The improvements in mechanical properties are lower than those obtained by the incorporation with functionalized diamond and nanocrystalline cellulose. For instance, addition of 1.5 wt% functionalized diamond to a PLA/maleic anhydride grafted styrene-ethylene/butylene-styrene copolymers (MAH-g-SEBS) blended film containing 3 wt% MAH-g-SEBS increased the tensile strength by 41.0% and elongation at break by 543.7% [13]. Nanocrystalline cellulose, on the other hand, increased the tensile strength by 17.85%, while decreased the elongation at break for the PLA/polyethylene glycol (PEG) composite film comprising 12 wt% PEG [47]. However, above enhancement effects are achieved by the synergistic effect of additional polymers with plasticization.

The fracture surface characteristics of pure PLA film, DDACDs/PLA (0.1 wt%) film and ODACDs/PLA (0.05 wt%) film were studied by SEM. As presented in Fig. 8 , the morphology of the tensile fractured surface for pure PLA film shows a smooth, flat, and low texture surface, suggesting the typical brittle fracture. However, oriented fibers filaments and tough cavities appeared in DDACDs/PLA and DDACDs/PLA film, which present the characteristics of toughness fracture [45]. To be specific, this type of oriented filament and cavities structure in the PLA matrix could consume more energy [13] when the DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA films are subjected to external force. These results confirm that DDACDs and ODACDs have a strong interface bonding with the PLA matrix, which changes the characteristics from brittle fracture to ductile fracture. In a word, the introduction of DDACDs and ODACDs, especially ODACDs, enhanced the toughness of PLA film, which is consistent with the analysis of mechanical performance.

Fig. S5 showed EDS images of the pure PLA film, DDACDs/PLA (0.1 wt%) film and ODACDs/PLA (0.05 wt%) film, indicating the appearance of N element in MCDs/PLA films due to the addition of MCDs. From the elemental mapping images given in Fig. S6, it can be found that the N element uniformly distributes in the PLA matrix, suggesting the uniform dispersion of MCDs in MCDs/PLA film.

The FTIR spectra of the films showed that the addition of MCDs cannot change the main chemical structure of PLA (Fig. S7), and thus the biodegradable property of MCDs/PLA films [35]. Similarly, the UV–vis transmission spectra in Fig. S8 illustrated that the light transmission of MCDs/PLA films cannot be affected by the addition of MCDs.

The crystallinity largely determines the mechanical performances of PLA-based films [48], and the introduction of DDACDs and ODACDs enhance the toughness of PLA film as described above, so the crystallinity of PLA film should also be improved. To verify the assumption, the crystallization behavior of DDACDs/PLA (0.1 wt%) film, ODACDs/PLA (0.05 wt%) film and pure PLA film were studied by DSC [49]. The crystallization behavior parameters including glass transition temperature (Tg), cold crystallization temperature (Tcc), melting temperature (Tm), cold crystallization enthalpy (∆Hcc), and melting enthalpy (∆Hm) of three films were shown in Fig. 9a. Xc was calculated according to eq. (1) and summarized in the inserted table of Fig. 9a. As for pure PLA film, its Xc of only 7.2% indicates almost amorphous PLA matrix, whereas the Xc of DDACDs/PLA film with 1 wt% addition is 33.01%, it is noteworthy that the Xc of ODACDs/PLA film with 0.05 wt% addition is 35.44%. The significant enhancement in crystallinity may be due to the excellent interfacial compatibility between the ODACDs and the PLA matrix and the heterogeneous nucleation of ODACDs [19]. Fig. 10 shows the crystallization process of PLA-based films at 130 °C, the spherulitic morphology of pure PLA film exhibited a typical black cross extinction structure, and the spherulite size gradually increased with the crystallization time. Compared with pure PLA film, MCDs/PLA films exhibited higher nucleation density and finer spherulite size within the same crystallization time, indicating that the introduction of MCDs promotes the crystallization of PLA [6], which is consistent with the DSC analysis. Similarly, biochar microspheres (BM), as a heterogeneous nucleating agent, increased the crystallinity of the PLA composite film to 42.26% with the addition of 5 wt% [50]. However, due to the poor compatibility between BM and PLA, the toughness of BM/PLA composite film is lower than that of pure PLA film.

As shown in Fig. 9b, the temperatures corresponding to the 5% mass loss rate for DDACDs/PLA (0.1 wt%) film and ODACDs/PLA (0.05 wt%) film increased to 321.18 °C and 320.18 °C, respectively, compared with that of 309.64 °C for pure PLA film, and similar phenomena also occur in the maximum thermal degradation temperatures. This may be because the strong interaction between MCDs and PLA improves the thermal stability of MCDs/PLA films [51]. In addition, the higher crystallinity of PLA composite film will also increase the energy barrier of thermal decomposition [12].

Combining all the above analysis, the crystallinity, toughness and thermal stability of PLA composite films are improved due to the good compatibility and strong interaction between MCDs and PLA, and the improvement effect was affected by the molecular chain length of organic amines used for CDs modification. In order to confirm this inference at molecular scale, simultaneous rheology and in-situ FTIR techniques and MD simulations will be used for further research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of the effect of MCDs on PLA at molecular scale
Simultaneous rheology and in-situ FTIR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 11a, the functional groups of different samples do not change much with time, and the characteristic absorption peaks around 1746 cm−1 and 1755 cm−1 are ascribed to the CO group of PLA in the amorphous and crystalline states, respectively [52]. 3D FTIR spectra in Fig. 11d, e and f show that the absorbance around 1755 cm−1 from both DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA have a significant increase, while that from pure PLA has no obvious change. In order to analyze the specific impact of the addition of MCDs to PLA, 2D infrared spectrum analysis was carried out, where red was defined as positive correlation, and blue was defined as negative correlation. There were two strong autocorrelation peaks at (1746 cm−1, 1746 cm−1) and (1755 cm−1, 1755 cm−1) in the synchronous 2D correlation spectrum (11 g, 11 h, 11i) of the three samples, indicating that the CO group had an obvious response to external interference (isothermal crystallization time). The difference is that the response of pure PLA at (1746 cm−1, 1746 cm−1) is more significant, whereas the responses of DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA at (1755 cm−1, 1755 cm−1) are more significant, suggesting that the addition of MCDs promotes the formation of the PLA crystalline state [52]. Since the cross peaks at (1755 cm−1, 1746 cm−1) and (1746 cm−1, 1755 cm−1) are both negatively correlated, the response of the CO group at 1746 cm−1 and 1755 cm−1 to time is opposite. In other words, the crystalline state increases while the amorphous state decreases with time, which is consistent with the transition from the amorphous to the crystalline state in the crystallization of PLA [53]. The asynchronous 2D correlation spectra in Fig. 11j, k, l show that the cross peaks of DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA at (1755 cm−1, 1746 cm−1) is negative correlation, while that of PLA at (1755 cm−1, 1746 cm−1) is positive correlation. According to Noda's rule, the enhanced response in MCDs/PLA preferentially occurs at 1755 cm−1 (crystalline state), while PLA changes preferentially at 1746 cm−1 (amorphous state), indicating that the addition of MCDs promotes the transition from amorphous to crystalline state in PLA [54]. The dramatic change in the carbonyl region of PLA after adding MCDs is caused by a strong interaction between the MCDs and PLA, which enhances the crystalline behavior of PLA. In addition, a characteristic peak corresponding to α-crystal appeared at 921 cm−1 [55] in the FTIR spectra of DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA compared to PLA (Fig. 11a), indicating that the MCDs promote the formation of α-crystal of PLA without altering its crystallization mechanism.

Fig. 11b shows the variation of energy storage modulus during isothermal crystallization, and the relative crystallinity of PLA, DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA (Fig. 11c) is calculated using energy storage modulus according to Eq. (2). As illustrated in Fig. 11c, the crystallization of DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA was almost completed at 32 min, while the crystallization time of PLA was 60 min. The above results suggest that the incorporation of MCDs can not only promote crystallization but also accelerate the crystallization rate of PLA.

MD simulation can study the effect of MCDs on PLA molecules at the molecular level, where the binding energy can measure the strength of the interaction between the filler and the polymer. According to Eq. (3), Ebinding is the negative value of the interaction energy (denoted as Einter), and Einter can be evaluated by the total energy of the composite and its corresponding components in the equilibrium state.

As shown in Table 1 , the binding energy changes in the order of ODACDs/PLA>DDACDs/PLA>CDs/PLA, indicating that the interaction and compatibility of MCDs with PLA are stronger than CDs. The stronger effect of ODACDs on PLA than that of DDACDs is probably due to the longer carbon chain and stronger entanglement of ODACDs, which is consistent with the result of the mechanical properties of PLA composite films.

The slope of the MSD curve reflects the mobility of the polymer chains, and its value determines the difficulty of the molecular chain movement [12,56]. The MSD of pure PLA as well as DDACDs/PLA and ODACDs/PLA composites were compared in Fig. 12 , their slope increase in the sequences of SlopeODACDs/PLA<SlopeDDACDs/PLA<SlopePLA. The result shows that the strong interaction between MCDs and PLA impedes the relative motion of PLA molecular chains, and the impeding ability of ODACDs is stronger than that of DDACDs. This is because MCDs can form entanglement points with the PLA, and ODACDs has a longer molecular chain and stronger entanglement on the PLA than DDACDs [57], resulting in higher thermal stability and mechanical properties of ODACDs/PLA films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic degradation behavior of PLA-based films

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, CDs were prepared by oxidation of weathered coal under mild conditions. FTIR, HRTEM, UV–vis, PL, and XPS analyses confirmed that weathered coal could be used as a good carbon precursor for directly synthesizing CDs with oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. Through surface modification of CDs with organic amines to synthesize MCDs for improving its interface interaction with PLA, and the modification conditions were optimized using the response surface methodology. Then MCDs/PLA composite films were prepared with MCDs as fillers and the performance of composite films was assessed. The analyses of mechanical properties, crystallinity, thermogravimetry and biodegradability indicate that the crystallinity, toughness, and thermal stability of PLA film were enhanced without altering their biodegradability by adding a very small amount of MCDs, which would expand the application fields of PLA. Simultaneous rheological and in-situ FTIR studies and MD simulations also revealed the structure-properties relationship between MCDs and PLA. The results showed that the strong interaction between MCDs and PLA reduced the mobility of PLA molecular chains, which contributed to the improvement of macroscopic mechanical properties and microscopic crystallization behavior. It is believed that these results will provide a basis for the design and fabrication of high-performance MCDs/PLA composites.

Yumiao Ma: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yikelamu Jilili: Data curation, Validation. Tengfei Shao: Data curation, Validation. Weijun Zhen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Resources.
